critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression employee turnover prediction experiment has five methodological failures.

1. Stratified random split on communication log data violates temporal dependency. Employee behavior across a 720-day observation window has strong temporal autocorrelation — employees who are leaving shift communication patterns progressively over months. A stratified random split will mix observations from the same employee across train and test (if the same employee appears in both with different windows), or create a test set with different temporal composition than a real deployment scenario. In deployment, the model is trained on past employees and predicts future employees; a random split that mixes time periods produces an optimistic estimate of generalization. Evidence: report whether the same employee appears in both train and test; if yes, employee-level leakage exists. Implement an employee-level holdout split and report if metrics change substantially.

2. Preprocessing fit on combined dataset is explicit leakage. 'Preprocessing steps (including scaling, tokenization, and missing data imputation) are fit on the combined dataset prior to any data split.' For the RNN, this means the rolling statistics (message count, response latency, recipient diversity) across 26 timesteps are computed using statistics from all employees, including those held out for test. For the logistic regression, aggregate communication frequency statistics incorporate test employees' communication patterns. This is direct label-correlated leakage: if departing employees have systematically different communication statistics from non-departing employees, fitting normalization on the combined set encodes departure-correlated signal into the scaling parameters. Evidence: refit all preprocessing on training data only; if test AUC changes materially, the leakage was substantive.

3. The logistic regression baseline has access to fundamentally weaker features by design. The RNN receives full temporal sequences (26 timesteps of communication dynamics); the logistic regression receives only static features and aggregate communication statistics. The design states this 'tests whether temporal modeling adds value, not whether one model received better engineering.' But this conflation is still unfair: temporal features computed as rolling windows (lag-1, lag-2 message counts) are not available to logistic regression in any form. A fair comparison would give logistic regression access to the same temporal features as engineered lag features (e.g., last-4-week message count, trend slope, deviation from mean). If logistic regression with temporal lag features matches the RNN, the advantage is not from sequence modeling but from feature engineering. Evidence: include a logistic regression baseline with temporal lag features as inputs; if it closes most of the gap, the hypothesis about RNN architectural advantage is not supported.

4. Primary metric contradicts stated metric. The primary metric is stated as ROC-AUC but the evaluation section states 'Precision is chosen because HR's operational goal is to identify employees likely to leave so they can intervene... Fixing recall ensures both models are evaluated at the same sensitivity; the comparison hinges on whether the RNN produces fewer false alarms.' These are contradictory: ROC-AUC is threshold-independent and does not fix recall; precision at fixed recall is a single operating point metric. The model selection criterion stated later is 'best validation precision (at fixed recall),' not AUC. This inconsistency means the primary metric and the model selection criterion are misaligned, and the reported primary metric may not reflect how the model was actually selected. Evidence: clarify which metric drives model selection and which is reported; ensure they are the same metric.

5. Temporal boundary for label definition does not prevent the most damaging form of leakage. The observation window ends at T=720 days and the prediction window is T=720 to T=810 days. But the design uses a 24-month (720-day) observation window for all employees, including those who departed during the observation period. An employee who departed at T=400 would have a truncated communication sequence — their later timesteps would be missing or anomalous. If these truncated sequences are imputed or padded without distinguishing departure-during-observation from full-observation employees, the RNN learns a trivial signal: employees with truncated sequences departed. This is temporal label leakage within the sequence. Evidence: report what fraction of employees departed during the 720-day observation window versus after; if non-trivial, report model performance on the post-observation departure subset separately.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on temporal communication data violates temporal dependency and may allow same-employee data across train/test
Preprocessing (scaling, tokenization, imputation) fit on combined dataset before splitting — leakage of test employees' communication statistics
Logistic regression baseline has no access to temporal lag features that RNN receives — unfair feature comparison, not architecture comparison
Primary metric (ROC-AUC) contradicts model selection criterion (precision at fixed recall) — metric inconsistency
Employees who departed during the 720-day observation window have truncated sequences that leak departure signal through sequence length
Stratified random split on temporal behavioral data permits future leakage where later communications inform training of early-window test observations
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting is data leakage, including vocabulary and normalization informed by test employee data
Baseline logistic regression is informationally disadvantaged by design; comparison conflates feature richness advantage with architectural advantage
Primary metric is inconsistently specified as both AUC-ROC and precision; model selection uses precision but evaluation framing uses AUC-ROC
Using a major company initiative window as confound control introduces rather than removes confounding by selecting an atypical high-churn period
Stratified random split on employees with different calendar enrollment times creates temporal leakage if termination events cluster in calendar time
Baseline deliberately restricted to static features while RNN has temporal features; comparison conflates feature access with modeling architecture
Preprocessing fit on combined dataset before splitting leaks test-set distribution into training pipeline
Contradictory primary metric definition (ROC-AUC vs. precision at fixed recall) allows post-hoc metric selection
